data.name: Dominik Livaković
data.description: Not even the most famous footballer in his (wider) family; through his mother, Manuela, he is related to Josip Skoblar, the 1971 European Golden Shoe winner, who scored 44 goals for Marseille that season. As Livakovic, who also played basketball as a kid and had enrolled at a university to study international relations, was making his way in senior football, his father, Zdravko – a politician and state secretary at the time – spent five months in detention for his alleged involvement in a big corruption case. In 2022 against Japan, Livakovic became only the third goalkeeper in World Cup history to save three penalties in a single shootout; then he also saved Rodrygo’s shot in another shootout against Brazil in the quarter-finals.

data.name: Josip Stanišić
data.description: The 26-year-old Munich-born versatile defender has won the league every season in his professional career since his debut in April 2021 and, while that may not sound so outlandish given he has always been under contract with Bayern, it is also worth noting that he did it in 2024 as well, when he was on loan to Bayer Leverkusen under Xabi Alonso. Stanisic played for Germany Under-19s but switched to Croatia and still very vividly remembers his first call-up. “We were in the car, the whole family, and the call was on speakers,” he recalls. “I laughed, couldn’t believe it, but then I saw my sister cry. My dad also cried, my mum cried too. It was one of the most beautiful moments in my life.”

data.name: Marin Pongračić
data.description: “Football was not my only priority,” the Germany-born defender, now 28, recently said in an interview. “I should have done things differently – eat better, go to bed on time, do some additional training in the gym … What I was missing the most was a fully professional approach and discipline,” he explained, talking about his earlier years and saying he has learned from his past mistakes. Pongracic had been going through a rough time in the Bundesliga and spoke publicly about his struggles, especially with Mark van Bommel, his coach at Wolfsburg. “I was going mad and wanted to have a go at Van Bommel. But that’s not how things are done, you have to sit with him and talk like a politician,” he said. Eventually he decided to move to Italy and he is now an important player for Fiorentina. With the national team he is still considered a bit of an outsider.

data.name: Joško Gvardiol
data.description: He is now one of the best defenders in the world, but at 16 Gvardiol wasn’t happy about how his development was going and even considered quitting football altogether and joining his father, Tihomir, in selling fish at the Dolac marketplace in Zagreb. In the end he took a mental map that a family friend made for him and hung it on the wall of his room. “SUCCESS,” it said in capital letters, while three other words were highlighted: discipline, top club and plan A. The Little Pep, as he’s been nicknamed from his very early days, has achieved it all, while remaining a remarkably down-to-earth guy who never lets fame get to his head. But what if things hadn’t worked out as a footballer, what would he have done instead? “Make pastries,” he quickly said when asked that question.

data.name: Duje Ćaleta-Car
data.description: Just in case you are wondering, “car” doesn’t mean automobile in Croatian; it actually means “emperor”. Having left home at 16 to join Red Bull Salzburg academy, he was the youngest member of the 2018 World Cup runners-up squad and developed into a strong, tough defender who is also confident on the ball. He is, however, prone to occasional blunders. Recently he conceded a penalty that media in Spain called “the most surreal in a 100 years”. Playing for Real Sociedad against Alavés in the Cup, he stopped the opposing forward Toni Martinez by pulling his shirt over his head, leaving the player to spin disoriented in the box. Alavés, however, missed the spot kick. Caleta-Car’s wife, Adriana, a former model and a law graduate, is a popular social media influencer.

data.name: Josip Šutalo
data.description: Only 14 when he left his Metkovic home to move 320 miles up north and join the Dinamo Zagreb youth academy, he is an exciting and quick ball-playing centre-back who is calm and highly professional on and off the pitch. For the national team he has been crucial in transforming the previously ageing Croatia backline. At the 2022 World Cup, the veteran Dejan Lovren was chosen ahead of him, but Sutalo has now amassed more than 30 caps, hardly ever disappointing. His move to Ajax in 2023 did not get off to the best start as he was among the players criticised for the team’s poor form but he has now made more than 100 appearances for the famous club and seem to have cemented his starting place in Zlatko Dalic’s side as well.

data.name: Nikola Moro
data.description: The defensive midfielder, who switched from Hajduk to their rivals Dinamo’s youth ranks at the age of 11, has an unusual and a bit old-fashioned hobby for a footballer. “I love enigmatics and particularly enjoy solving crossword puzzles,” he says. Despite representing – and usually captaining – Croatia at every age-group level, as well as being one of the best players in the domestic league for years, it took a long time for him to break into the senior national team. He got his chance in a 2022 friendly against Bulgaria when he was 24 and another one in late 2024. Strangely, he is still on the margins despite being a regular for high-flying Bologna for years.

data.name: Mateo Kovačić
data.description: “My game is to open space for others,” Kovacic once said, adding that it is “a big strength” for him. Kovacic is often in Luka Modric’s shadow and not always fully appreciated for his contribution, his value being best noticed at times of his absence, so fans in Croatia are hopeful he’ll make a complete recovery from injury in time for the World Cup and be at his best once again. A devout Catholic, sometimes referred to as the Altar Boy – because, well, he used to be one and met his future wife while she was in the choir. He is a serial winner and he has collected four Champions League titles (three with Real Madrid, one with Chelsea) despite playing in only one final, and that was for 10 minutes.

data.name: Andrej Kramarić
data.description: For 16 years Kramaric took the same tram from his Zagreb home to the Maksimir stadium, where he trained with Dinamo, having started at the age of six. He was obsessed with goals and, by his own count, scored 452 of them in official matches for various age categories. And yet his career only really took off when he moved to Rijeka and particularly after embracing a more withdrawn role, thriving between the lines. Kramaric is a forgotten member of Leicester’s 2016 Premier League-winning side, having been loaned out to Hoffenheim halfway through that season. A decade later, he is still there, and now also one of Croatia senior men, having scored 36 goals in his 114 appearances at the time of writing, looking for his World Cup swan song.

data.name: Luka Modrić
data.description: After two decades with the national team, the once-in-a-generation player is closing in on 200 caps – a ridiculous number. The captain has still got it, at the age of 40, and has been a key player for Milan this past season. The national-team coach, Zlatko Dalic, calls him “the engine of this team”. Also the most-decorated player in Real Madrid’s history, having won 28 trophies despite only joining them shortly before his 27th birthday. Modric really is larger-than-life and his personality is complex and layered, from the war child-cum-Ballon d’Or story to his legal troubles. “What people don’t realise about Luka,” said the late Tomislav Basic, one of Modric’s early coaches whom the player called his second father, “is that he is largely self-taught. For many days and months, this under-nourished, small kid played with the ball by himself in a parking lot by a refugee hotel, banging it against the wall and then controlling it.” This was where his trademark moves, like the outside-of-the-foot trivela or the half-turn when receiving the ball, came to be.

data.name: Ante Budimir
data.description: A true late bloomer who became an Osasuna legend despite joining them only at the age of 29 – his Croatia debut came a few months later – Budimir has sometimes been described as a “poor man’s Mario Mandzukic”, mainly for his hard work off the ball and strong presence in the air. A war refugee from Bosnia, he is also a really nice guy and a university student of economics and business who lists Roberto Benigni’s La Vita è Bella as his favourite film. Soon 35, he may finally be Croatia’s first-choice up front. Oh, yes – he’s also the protagonist of one of the worst penalties ever taken in football, the bizarre effort against Valencia that caused mass facepalming on the home bench and in the crowd. Just google it.

data.name: Ivor Pandur
data.description: Most fans in Croatia will only vaguely remember the goalkeeper whose surname is one of the slang terms for a policeman; he left his native Rijeka after just one season in senior football and has since been playing abroad – for Verona, Fortuna Sittard and now Hull City. And although he was part of every national youth squad category from under-15s to under-21s, at the time of writing he is yet to make his debut for the senior Croatia team. Pandur is a yoga practitioner and a religious person, and his views on the Championship, the league where he currently plays, are rather interesting. “It’s brutal, a bit like rugby, it makes you tougher,” he says. “In every game you get punched with either an elbow or a knee. As long as I’m healthy, I’m enjoying it.”

data.name: Nikola Vlašić
data.description: Described as “a motor monster” by his own dad, Josko, a former decathlete, Nikola was a well-known talent in his country years before 2014, when he was included in the Guardian’s inaugural Next Generation list. His older sister, Blanka, was a world-conquering athlete in high jump and Josko, a renowned fitness coach, worked with Nikola from the age of four. “We played everywhere,” Vlasic Sr said. “Even in the living room or in the elevator … I’d never let him lose. Once I accidentally beat him in a game and he cried a river of tears.” Failed to make his mark in England on two separate occasions – with Everton and West Ham – but is an important player for Torino and has scored some crucial goals for Croatia over his 60 appearances.

data.name: Ivan Perišić
data.description: While it’s true that Croatia have been Luka Modric’s team for nearly 20 years, Perisic has arguably been just as important for them for most of that time. Now 37, he has had a brilliant season for PSV, joining them after fulfilling his dream of playing for his childhood club, Hajduk Split, whom he had to leave at age 17 in order to save his father’s chicken farm from going bust. Perisic played in Belgium, Germany, Italy, England and now the Netherlands, ever-evolving as a player, even after a bad cruciate ligament injury that threatened to end his career. This is surely his last big tournament and he is still at full strength.

data.name: Mario Pašalić
data.description: At the age of 17 Pasalic had the world at his feet. He was considered a top talent and a move abroad seemed only a matter of time. Then, suddenly, his upward trajectory was stopped by a staph infection and he had to spend the next six months in hospital. “At times I thought I wouldn’t be able to continue playing,” he said. A year later, however, he scored both goals for Hajduk in a derby win against Dinamo and he was signed by Chelsea the following summer. After five loan spells and not a single appearance for the Blues, he found a home at Atalanta, where he is now a legend after eight years at the club. Pasalic is often Croatia’s joker card, filling in wherever Zlatko Dalic needs him.

data.name: Martin Baturina
data.description: He did gymnastics training for seven years before deciding to focus on football. Once a top talent in the Hajduk Split academy before his dad, the Croatian league legend Mate Baturina, controversially decided to move him and his older brother, Roko, who now plays in Denmark, to their bitter rivals Dinamo. “I was just a kid and it wasn’t my call,” he says. Wonderfully gifted but never consistent enough and perhaps not appreciating the importance of tracking back, Baturina used to be seen as a bit of an old-school advanced playmaker – a classic Balkans No 10, if you will – who might not have what it takes to succeed in today’s football. However, he has matured playing under Cesc Fàbregas this season at Como and has also proven his value for Croatia.

data.name: Petar Sučić
data.description: “I prefer the real life,” the 22-year-old Inter midfielder recently said when asked why he doesn’t use any social media. Growing up on a farm, he said he always loved being outdoors. “I had a very happy childhood and liked that lifestyle. I’d help my family milk cows and that was fun.” When Sucic made his debut for Dinamo less than three years ago, he was a little-known outsider who played for Bosnia and Herzegovina’s youth teams and no one expected much from him. But within a year he had not only established himself in the first team but also switched to representing Croatia and was called up for the senior squad. At the end of the following season, he was sold to Inter and is now a starter for club and country, where he is seen as a successor to the iron-lunged Marcelo Brozovic.

data.name: Kristijan Jakić
data.description: You could argue that Jakic is Croatia’s footballing Peter Pan, an instantly likeable character who never really grew up. He collects Pokemon stickers, Hot Wheels toy cars and famous players’ shirts, while his many tattoos include tributes to his grandparents as well as roses, tears, a gladiator and a tiger. He says he never liked school and enjoyed farming the land with his grandad much more. “I was 14 when I first drove a tractor. I enjoyed making wine and planting potatoes. I still enjoy that even now when I visit my family,” he says. Now at Augsburg in the Bundesliga, he was Croatia’s unlikely hero in the qualifiers, playing right-back because of an injury crisis. His natural home is in defensive midfield.

data.name: Toni Fruk
data.description: The only player still in the domestic league but that is not to say that he doesn’t have experience from playing abroad, having first joined Mouscron in Belgium at age 17 and then Fiorentina’s Primavera side. The talent was never in question, but his development had been hampered by a “mysterious” injury that caused him severe joint pain, while the club doctors insisted it was “all in his head”. He took a step back and returned to Croatia, eventually becoming the top flight’s star, making it to the national team and is now waiting for another move abroad. A creative attacking midfielder or a false 9 who could be Croatia’s secret weapon at the World Cup, he started his career as a goalkeeper.

data.name: Igor Matanović
data.description: "Croats, war refugees who had nothing and didn’t even speak the language.” He recalls a visit to the bombed and abandoned house where his mother’s family used to live. “Among the ruins, there was still an album with photos of Michael Jackson that she collected. They were just teenagers and suddenly they needed to start a whole new life.” Born in Hamburg, Matanovic opted to play for Croatia and is still making a name for himself in the Bundesliga and with the national team. In his hometown, he owns an Oakberry franchise that sells acai bowls and smoothies, which has its benefits: “I love that feeling when you just go to the counter, take what you want and don’t have to pay for it.”

data.name: Luka Sučić
data.description: The jury is still out on the Real Sociedad midfielder, with the main question being whether he can become Croatia’s new creative leader once Modric and Kovacic retire. Now 23, he has had more than his share of ups and downs, at times looking like a true footballing sensation while in others he has struggled with injuries and managerial changes. He definitely has something special and has shown it on many occasions but now he needs to be more consistent to truly progress. The midfielder is actually a cousin to Petar Sucic, although neither was born in Croatia; Luka grew up in Austria but played for Croatia as early as the under-15s and does not even hold Austrian citizenship.

data.name: Luka Vušković
data.description: Two days after his 16th birthday, he made his senior debut for Hajduk Split in a derby against Dinamo; a week later, he scored his first goal. He has always been effortlessly dominant among older players, such that his Croatia debut at the age of 18 already seemed overdue. A true force not only in defence but also in buildup play and in attack, he scored seven goals in Belgium last season and five for Hamburg since last summer; among them the 2025 Bundesliga goal of the year, a spectacular scorpion kick finish. Coming from a multigenerational football family – his brother Mario is also at Hamburg – Vuskovic is now finally ready to introduce himself at the big stage and show why everyone thinks he’ll be a superstar for many years to come. Tottenham signed him for a bargain £10m in 2025 and surely they won’t loan him out again?

data.name: Dominik Kotarski
data.description: “I have no regrets,” says the 26-year-old, who has had a somewhat peculiar career path. “Things that didn’t happen are not worth thinking or talking about.” A product of Dinamo Zagreb's academy, to which he came from a lower-league side, Tondach Bedekovcina, he refused to sign a professional deal, instead moving to Ajax before turning 18. Four years later, he was back in Croatia at HNK Gorica, without ever appearing for Dinamo, Ajax or any other club (he did play for Jong Ajax, the Dutch team’s reserve side). A really good season there earned him a move to Paok in Greece and then, amid rumours of interest from various big European clubs, he chose FC Copenhagen as his next destination.

data.name: Marco Pašalić
data.description: After graduating from high school Pasalic’s dreams of becoming a professional footballer still looked a bit distant. So after training at his hometown club, Karlsruhe, he would work at construction sites for his brother’s company, installing flooring or completing interior designs, sometimes even on graveyard shifts until 3am or 4am. He was also a hardcore supporter of the Vatreni, who travelled across the continent to see them play. “To the other workers I’d say: ‘One day I will play for the Croatian national team,'” he recalls. Spells with Stuttgart and Dortmund’s reserve sides followed and Pasalic was already 21 when he made his “proper” senior debut. But what a debut it was, for Dortmund in the DFL-Supercup Klassiker against Bayern (he played only 12 minutes, but still). After just one further appearance, he joined Rijeka in 2023 and was called up for Croatia within three months before being sold to Orlando last year.

data.name: Martin Erlić
data.description: Only a few days after playing in the 2023 Nations League final against Spain, Erlic was in the field helping out at his family farm. “When I was a child, we lived under the same roof with farm animals, only a wall separated us,” he once said in a revealing interview. “There were hard times and we collected bottles to sell them … no shame in that though,” he added. Erlic left his country for Italy at the age of 16 and struggled with many club changes, loans and injuries. When he made his senior international debut in June 2022, the call-up was so unexpected that he had to miss his brother’s wedding in order to play. “I felt sorry,” he says. “But this is what all the training and the sacrifices I made have come down to.”

data.name: Petar Musa
data.description: The Moose, as they like to call him there, is pretty big in Texas these days. He’s known for his scoring and a goal celebration that includes putting his open palms with fingers spread to the side of his head, imitating antlers. In Croatia, though, it took him a long time to be taken seriously and as a genuine option up front; he never played for a big club at home and moved abroad aged 19 without having scored a senior goal. That has changed; after seven years in Czechia, Germany and Portugal – including Champions League experience with Benfica – he signed for FC Dallas in 2024 and has developed into one of MLS’s leading strikers. His mother, Jagoda Obradovic, played for the women’s national team in the mid-1990s.
